The long-term success of moving in together depends on the development of a shared daily routine. Beyond the initial setup, couples must navigate the ongoing maintenance of the household and the social dynamics of their new neighborhood. This involves a shift from 'searching' vocabulary to 'living' vocabulary, focusing on chores, bills, and community interactions.

We explore Daily Life Vocabulary to help you discuss cleaning schedules, grocery lists, and home repairs. Additionally, the section on Meeting the Neighbors provides the social scripts and etiquette required to introduce yourselves to the community and manage the common courtesies of apartment or neighborhood life.

vizinho neighbor

Pronunciation: vee-zee-nyoo

"O nosso vizinho do andar de cima é muito simpático."

Daily Life Vocabulary

  • I'm lar! - Arriving home
  • Let's clean juntos - Sharing chores
  • The vizinhos are nice - Social observation
  • Our lar is beautiful - Appreciation
🇵🇹

Household Responsibilities

Discuss how household chores are traditionally divided in Portuguese-speaking households. Open communication about expectations helps create harmony in your new lar.

Meeting the Neighbors

Portuguese English Situation
Hello, vizinhos Hello, neighbors Introduction
We just moved in We just moved in Explanation
Nice to meet you Nice to meet you Greeting
We live juntos We live together Clarification

Quick Reference Card

Need Vocabulary
Finding home apartamento, casa, aluguel
Moving day mudança, móveis, desempacotar
New home chaves, lar, juntos
Community vizinhos

Building a lar juntos in two languages creates a unique blend of cultures. Every piece of móveis you choose, every room you organize, becomes part of your beautiful multicultural life.

For more relationship vocabulary, explore our essential Portuguese phrases for couples guide.

title: Portuguese Numbers and Counting
content_html:
Numbers are essential for daily life. From telling your partner when you will arrive home to shopping and understanding prices, Portuguese numbers are a fundamental building block. The good news is that once you learn the patterns, counting becomes straightforward.

Numbers 1-10

💕

Phrase to Learn

Eu te amo um milhão de vezes

I love you a million times

[ EH-oo chee AH-moo oom mee-LYOWN deh VEH-zeez ]

A romantic way to use large numbers to express infinite love

Um, dois, três One, two, three

Pronunciation: oom, doysh, trehsh

"Tenho três irmãos. (I have three siblings.)"

Quatro, cinco, seis Four, five, six

Pronunciation: KWAH-troo, SEEN-koo, saysh

"Cinco minutos, por favor. (Five minutes, please.)"

Sete, oito, nove, dez Seven, eight, nine, ten

Pronunciation: SEH-chee, OY-too, NOH-vee, dehz

"São dez horas. (It is ten o'clock.)"

Numbers 11-20

🇧🇷

Gender Agreement

The numbers one and two change based on gender. Use 'um/dois' for masculine nouns and 'uma/duas' for feminine nouns. For example: um livro (one book) but uma mesa (one table).

Onze, doze, treze Eleven, twelve, thirteen

Pronunciation: ON-zee, DOH-zee, TREH-zee

"Tenho doze anos de casado. (I have been married for twelve years.)"

Catorze, quinze, dezesseis Fourteen, fifteen, sixteen

Pronunciation: kah-TOR-zee, KEEN-zee, deh-zeh-SAYSH

"Quinze de novembro é feriado. (November 15th is a holiday.)"

Dezessete, dezoito, dezenove, vinte Seventeen, eighteen, nineteen, twenty

Pronunciation: deh-zeh-SEH-chee, deh-ZOY-too, deh-zeh-NOH-vee, VEEN-chee

"Ela tem dezenove anos. (She is nineteen years old.)"

Tens and Larger Numbers

Trinta, quarenta, cinquenta Thirty, forty, fifty

Pronunciation: TREEN-tah, kwah-REN-tah, seen-KWEN-tah

"Custa cinquenta reais. (It costs fifty reais.)"

Sessenta, setenta, oitenta, noventa Sixty, seventy, eighty, ninety

Pronunciation: seh-SEN-tah, seh-TEN-tah, oy-TEN-tah, noh-VEN-tah

"Minha avó tem oitenta anos. (My grandmother is eighty years old.)"

Cem, cento e um One hundred, one hundred and one

Pronunciation: sayn, SEN-too ee oom

"Cento e cinquenta pessoas vieram. (One hundred fifty people came.)"

Large Numbers

Duzentos, trezentos, quatrocentos Two hundred, three hundred, four hundred

Pronunciation: doo-ZEN-toosh, treh-ZEN-toosh, kwah-troo-SEN-toosh

"O apartamento custa trezentos mil. (The apartment costs three hundred thousand.)"

Mil One thousand

Pronunciation: meel

"Dois mil e vinte e seis. (Two thousand twenty-six.)"

Um milhão, um bilhão One million, one billion

Pronunciation: oom mee-LYOWN, oom bee-LYOWN

"O Brasil tem mais de duzentos milhões de habitantes. (Brazil has more than two hundred million inhabitants.)"

Telling Time

🇵🇹

24-Hour Clock

Portuguese-speaking countries commonly use the 24-hour clock in formal contexts. You might hear "às quinze horas" (at 15:00) instead of "às três da tarde" (at 3 PM).

Que horas são? What time is it?

Pronunciation: kee OH-rahz sown

"Amor, que horas são? (Love, what time is it?)"

São duas horas It is two o'clock

Pronunciation: sown DOO-ahz OH-rahz

"São duas horas da tarde. (It is two in the afternoon.)"

É uma hora It is one o'clock

Pronunciation: eh OO-mah OH-rah

"É uma hora da manhã."

Meia-noite / Meio-dia Midnight / Noon

Pronunciation: MAY-ah NOY-chee / MAY-oo JEE-ah

"Nos encontramos ao meio-dia. (We will meet at noon.)"

Dates

Qual é a data de hoje? What is today's date?

Pronunciation: kwahl eh ah DAH-tah deh OH-zhee

"Hoje é dia quinze de janeiro. (Today is January 15th.)"

O primeiro de janeiro January 1st

Pronunciation: oo pree-MAY-roo deh zhah-NAY-roo

"O primeiro de janeiro é feriado nacional."

Romantic Number Phrases

Já faz dois anos que estamos juntos We have been together for two years now

Pronunciation: zhah fahz doysh AH-nohs kee esh-TAH-moosh ZHOON-toosh

"Amor, já faz dois anos que estamos juntos. (Love, we have been together for two years now.)"

Você é meu número um You are my number one

Pronunciation: voh-SEH eh MEH-oo NOO-meh-roo oom

"Na minha vida, você é meu número um. (In my life, you are my number one.)"

Mil beijos para você A thousand kisses for you

Pronunciation: meel BEH-zhoosh PAH-rah voh-SEH

"Boa noite, amor. Mil beijos para você. (Good nightlearn/en/pt/portuguese-good-morning-goodnight-phrases/">Good night, love. A thousand kisses for you.)"

Practice counting during daily activities. Count steps while walking, count items while shopping, and tell your partner the time in Portuguese. Regular practice makes numbers automatic.

title: Portuguese Past Tense for Couples: Pretérito vs Imperfeito
content_html:
Understanding Portuguese past tensesf="/learn/en/pt/portuguese-grammar-basics-for-beginners/">tenses is essential for sharing memories with your partner, telling stories about your relationship, and discussing your history together. The two main past tenses—pretérito perfeito (simple past) and pretérito imperfeito (imperfect past)—work together to paint vivid pictures of the past.

The Big Picture: When to Use Each Tense

💕

Phrase to Learn

Quando te vi, me apaixonei

When I saw you, I fell in love

[ KWAHN-doo chee vee, mee ah-pai-sho-NAY ]

Both verbs use pretérito perfeito for completed actions

Think of Portuguese past tenses this way:

  • Pretérito Perfeito: Completed actions with a clear beginning and end ("I met you," "We kissed," "She said yes")
  • Pretérito Imperfeito: Ongoing states, habits, or background descriptions ("I was nervous," "We used to walk," "She was beautiful")
🇵🇹

The Storytelling Secret

Portuguese speakers masterfully blend both tenses when telling stories. The imperfeito sets the scene (background), while the perfeito moves the action forward (events). Listen to your partner tell stories and you'll hear this rhythm naturally!

Pretérito Perfeito: Completed Actions

Use this tense for specific events that happened and finished in the past.

Regular -AR Verbs (like "amar" - to love)

amar (pretérito perfeito)

to love (simple past)

eu amei I loved
tu amaste you loved (informal)
você/ele/ela amou you/he/she loved
nós amamos we loved
vocês/eles/elas amaram you all/they loved
Eu te amei desde o primeiro momento I loved you from the first moment

Pronunciation: EH-oo chee ah-MAY DEHZ-jee oo pree-MAY-roo moh-MEHN-too

"Amor, eu te amei desde o primeiro momento que te vi. (Love, I loved you from the first moment I saw you.)"

Regular -ER Verbs (like "conhecer" - to meet/know)

conhecer (pretérito perfeito)

to meet/know (simple past)

eu conheci I met/knew
tu conheceste you met/knew
você/ele/ela conheceu you/he/she met/knew
nós conhecemos we met/knew
vocês/eles/elas conheceram you all/they met/knew
Onde você conheceu seu marido? Where did you meet your husband?

Pronunciation: OHN-jee voh-SEH koh-nyeh-SEH-oo SEH-oo mah-REE-doo

"Conheci meu amor na universidade. (I met my love at university.)"

Regular -IR Verbs (like "decidir" - to decide)

decidir (pretérito perfeito)

to decide (simple past)

eu decidi I decided
tu decidiste you decided
você/ele/ela decidiu you/he/she decided
nós decidimos we decided
vocês/eles/elas decidiram you all/they decided
Decidimos nos casar We decided to get married

Pronunciation: deh-see-JEE-moosh noosh kah-ZAR

"Ontem, decidimos nos casar! (Yesterday, we decided to get married!)"

Pretérito Imperfeito: Ongoing Past States

Use this tense for:

  • Habitual actions in the past
  • Descriptions and background information
  • Ongoing states or conditions
  • Age and time expressions

Regular -AR Verbs

amar (pretérito imperfeito)

to love (imperfect)

eu amava I loved/used to love/was loving
tu amavas you loved/used to love
você/ele/ela amava you/he/she loved/used to love
nós amávamos we loved/used to love
vocês/eles/elas amavam you all/they loved/used to love
Eu te amava em silêncio I loved you in silence / I was loving you silently

Pronunciation: EH-oo chee ah-MAH-vah ayn see-LEHN-see-oo

"Antes de te contar, eu te amava em silêncio. (Before I told you, I loved you in silence.)"

Regular -ER Verbs

querer (pretérito imperfeito)

to want (imperfect)

eu queria I wanted/used to want
tu querias you wanted/used to want
você/ele/ela queria you/he/she wanted
nós queríamos we wanted/used to want
vocês/eles/elas queriam you all/they wanted
Eu sempre queria te ver I always wanted to see you

Pronunciation: EH-oo SEHM-pree keh-REE-ah chee vehr

"Quando éramos jovens, eu sempre queria te ver. (When we were young, I always wanted to see you.)"

Regular -IR Verbs

sentir (pretérito imperfeito)

to feel (imperfect)

eu sentia I felt/used to feel
tu sentias you felt/used to feel
você/ele/ela sentia you/he/she felt
nós sentíamos we felt/used to feel
vocês/eles/elas sentiam you all/they felt
Eu sentia borboletas no estômago I felt butterflies in my stomach

Pronunciation: EH-oo sehn-CHEE-ah bor-boh-LEH-tahs noo esh-TOH-mah-goo

"Toda vez que te via, eu sentia borboletas. (Every time I saw you, I felt butterflies.)"

Essential Irregular Verbs

Some of the most romantic verbs are irregular. Here are the key ones:

Ser (to be) - Irregular in Both Tenses

ser (pretérito perfeito)

to be (simple past)

eu fui I was
tu foste you were
você/ele/ela foi you/he/she was
nós fomos we were
vocês/eles/elas foram you all/they were

ser (pretérito imperfeito)

to be (imperfect)

eu era I was/used to be
tu eras you were/used to be
você/ele/ela era you/he/she was
nós éramos we were/used to be
vocês/eles/elas eram you all/they were
Você era tão linda naquele dia You were so beautiful that day

Pronunciation: voh-SEH EH-rah TOWN LEEN-dah nah-KEH-lee JEE-ah

"No nosso casamento, você era tão linda. (At our wedding, you were so beautiful.)"

Ter (to have) - Irregular in Both Tenses

ter (pretérito perfeito)

to have (simple past)

eu tive I had
tu tiveste you had
você/ele/ela teve you/he/she had
nós tivemos we had
vocês/eles/elas tiveram you all/they had
Tivemos uma noite maravilhosa We had a wonderful night

Pronunciation: chee-VEH-moosh OO-mah NOY-chee mah-rah-vee-LYOH-zah

"Ontem tivemos uma noite maravilhosa juntos. (Yesterday we had a wonderful night together.)"

Ir (to go) - Identical to Ser in Pretérito Perfeito

Fomos ao restaurante onde nos conhecemos We went to the restaurant where we met

Pronunciation: FOH-moosh ow hesh-tow-RAHN-chee OHN-jee noosh koh-nyeh-SEH-moosh

"No nosso aniversário, fomos ao restaurante onde nos conhecemos. (On our anniversary, we went to the restaurant where we met.)"

🇵🇹

Context is Everything

"Fui," "foi," "fomos," etc. can mean either "was/were" (ser) or "went" (ir). Context always makes it clear which is intended!

Brazilian vs European Portuguese Differences

Concept Brazilian Portuguese European Portuguese
"We used to go" A gente ia Nós íamos
"I had loved" Eu tinha amado Eu tinha amado
"You were" (informal) Você era Tu eras

In Brazil, a gente (literally "the people") is commonly used instead of nós for "we," and it conjugates like ele/ela:

A gente se amava muito We loved each other a lot

Pronunciation: ah ZHEHN-chee see ah-MAH-vah MOON-too

"Naquela época, a gente se amava muito. (At that time, we loved each other a lot.)"

Combining Both Tenses: Telling Your Love Story

The magic happens when you use both tenses together:

Eu estava nervoso quando te pedi em casamento I was nervous when I asked you to marry me

Pronunciation: EH-oo esh-TAH-vah nehr-VOH-zoo KWAHN-doo chee peh-JEE ayn kah-zah-MEHN-too

"Eu estava nervoso quando te pedi em casamento. (I was nervous when I asked you to marry me.)"

Chovia quando nos beijamos pela primeira vez It was raining when we kissed for the first time

Pronunciation: shoh-VEE-ah KWAHN-doo noosh bay-ZHAH-moosh PEH-lah pree-MAY-rah vehz

"Chovia quando nos beijamos pela primeira vez. (It was raining when we kissed for the first time.)"

Você usava um vestido azul quando te conheci You were wearing a blue dress when I met you

Pronunciation: voh-SEH oo-ZAH-vah oom vesh-CHEE-doo ah-ZOOL KWAHN-doo chee koh-nyeh-SEE

"Você usava um vestido azul quando te conheci. (You were wearing a blue dress when I met you.)"

Practice Sentences for Couples

Try telling your partner about your relationship milestones:

Lembra quando nos conhecemos? Remember when we met?

Pronunciation: LEHM-brah KWAHN-doo noosh koh-nyeh-SEH-moosh

"Amor, lembra quando nos conhecemos? Eu estava tão nervoso! (Love, remember when we met? I was so nervous!)"

Eu sempre pensava em você I always thought about you / I was always thinking about you

Pronunciation: EH-oo SEHM-pree pehn-SAH-vah ayn voh-SEH

"Antes de namorarmos, eu sempre pensava em você. (Before we dated, I always thought about you.)"

O dia que você disse 'sim' foi o melhor da minha vida The day you said 'yes' was the best of my life

Pronunciation: oo JEE-ah kee voh-SEH JEE-see seem foy oo meh-LYOR dah MEE-nyah VEE-dah

"O dia que você disse 'sim' foi o melhor da minha vida. (The day you said 'yes' was the best of my life.)"

Quick Reference: Key Differences

Pretérito Perfeito Pretérito Imperfeito
Completed actions Ongoing states
Specific events Habitual actions
"I met you" "I used to see you"
"We kissed" "We were dating"
"She said yes" "She was happy"

Your Past Tense Journey

Mastering these tenses opens up a whole new world of conversation with your Portuguese-speaking partner. You'll be able to reminisce about how you met, share stories from your past, and create beautiful narratives of your life together.

Start by practicing the regular verbs, then gradually add the irregular ones. Before you know it, you'll be telling your love story in Portuguese with confidence!

🇵🇹

Love Stories in Portuguese

Portuguese and Brazilian love songs are perfect for hearing these tenses in action. Listen to romantic música popular brasileira (MPB) or fado from Portugal—you'll hear the pretérito perfeito and imperfeito woven together beautifully.

title: 50+ Portuguese Pet Names to Delight Your Partner
content_html:
Portuguese is often called one of the most romantic languages in the world, and nowhere is this more evident than in the endless variety of sweet names that Portuguese speakers use for their loved ones. Whether your partner is from Brazil or Portugal, learning these terms of endearment will add a special tenderness to your relationship.

The Most Commonearn/en/pt/portuguese-moving-in-together/">Most Commonearn/en/pt/portuguese-honeymoon-travel-phrases/">Most Commonearn/en/pt/portuguese-food-and-restaurant-vocabulary/">Most Commonearn/en/pt/portuguese-100-common-phrases-for-english-speakers/">Most Common Pet Names

💕

Phrase to Learn

Meu amor

My love

[ MEH-oo ah-MOR ]

The most universal and beloved term of endearment in Portuguese

Meu amor is the gold standard of Portuguese pet names. You'll hear it everywhere—from couples on the streets of Rio to families in Lisbon. It's appropriate in any context and never loses its warmth, no matter how often it's used.

Minha vida My life

Pronunciation: MEE-nyah VEE-dah

"Como você está, minha vida? (How are you, my life?)"

This beautiful expression shows just how much your partner means to you—they're not just part of your life, they ARE your life.

Meu bem My darling / My dear

Pronunciation: MEH-oo bayn

"Bom dia, meu bem! (Good morningarn/en/pt/portuguese-good-morning-goodnight-phrases/">Good morning, my darling!)"

Sweet Diminutives: Making It Even Cuter

🇵🇹

The Art of Diminutives

Portuguese speakers love diminutives (-inho/-inha endings). They transform ordinary words into something sweeter and more intimate. Adding -inho to almost any word makes it cuter and more affectionate!

Portuguese has a wonderful feature: you can make almost any word more affectionate by adding -inho (masculine) or -inha (feminine). Watch how it transforms these pet names:

Amorzinho Little love / Sweetheart

Pronunciation: ah-mor-ZEE-nyoo

"Vem cá, amorzinho. (Come here, sweetheart.)"

Benzinho Little darling

Pronunciation: bayn-ZEE-nyoo

"Benzinho, você esqueceu seu casaco. (Little darling, you forgot your coat.)"

Coração / Coraçãozinho Heart / Little heart

Pronunciation: koh-rah-SOWN / koh-rah-sown-ZEE-nyoo

"Meu coraçãozinho, te amo tanto. (My little heart, I love you so much.)"

Animal-Inspired Terms of Endearment

Like many languages, Portuguese borrows from the animal kingdom for affectionate nicknames:

Gatinho / Gatinha Kitten

Pronunciation: gah-CHEE-nyoo / gah-CHEE-nyah

"Oi, gatinha! Tudo bem? (Hey, kitten! How are you?)"

🇵🇹

Gatinha: A Brazilian Favorite

In Brazil, calling someone "gatinha" or "gatinho" is extremely popular. It implies they're attractive and desirable. While it can be flirty, between couples it's a sweet everyday term.

Ursinho / Ursinha Little bear

Pronunciation: oor-ZEE-nyoo / oor-ZEE-nyah

"Meu ursinho, vem me abraçar. (My little bear, come give me a hug.)"

Passarinho Little bird

Pronunciation: pah-sah-REE-nyoo

"Bom dia, passarinho! (Good morning, little bird!)"

Brazilian vs European Portuguese Pet Names

While many terms of endearment are shared, each variety has its favorites:

Brazilian Favorites

Neném / Nenê Baby

Pronunciation: neh-NAYN / neh-NEH

"Neném, já jantou? (Baby, have you had dinner?)"

Mozão / Mozinha Babe (from 'meu amor')

Pronunciation: moh-ZOWN / moh-ZEE-nyah

"E aí, mozão, vamos sair? (Hey babe, shall we go out?)"

This is a very casual, modern Brazilian term derived from shortening "meu amor" (my love).

European Portuguese Favorites

Querido / Querida Dear / Darling

Pronunciation: keh-REE-doo / keh-REE-dah

"Querida, posso ajudar-te? (Darling, can I help you?)"

Fofo / Fofa Cutie / Cute one

Pronunciation: FOH-foo / FOH-fah

"Estás tão fofo hoje! (You're so cute today!)"

Playful and Creative Names

Portuguese speakers get creative with their affection:

Docinho Little sweet / Sweetie

Pronunciation: doh-SEE-nyoo

"Meu docinho, te espero em casa. (My sweetie, I'll wait for you at home.)"

Flor Flower

Pronunciation: flohr

"Bom dia, minha flor! (Good morning, my flower!)"

Princesa / Príncipe Princess / Prince

Pronunciation: preen-SEH-zah / PREEN-see-pee

"Minha princesa, o jantar está pronto. (My princess, dinner is ready.)"

Lindinho / Lindinha Beautiful one

Pronunciation: leen-JEE-nyoo / leen-JEE-nyah

"Lindinha, você está maravilhosa. (Beautiful, you look wonderful.)"

Terms for Special Moments

Meu anjo My angel

Pronunciation: MEH-oo AHN-zhoo

"Meu anjo, obrigado por tudo. (My angel, thank you for everything.)"

Minha estrela My star

Pronunciation: MEE-nyah esh-TREH-lah

"Você é minha estrela, sabia? (You're my star, you know?)"

Meu tesouro My treasure

Pronunciation: MEH-oo teh-ZOH-roo

"Meu tesouro, te amo para sempre. (My treasure, I love you forever.)"

Using Pet Names Naturally

🇵🇹

When to Use Pet Names

In Portuguese-speaking cultures, pet names are used much more freely than in English. It's completely normal to hear couples use "amor" multiple times in a single conversation. Don't be shy about using them frequently—your partner will love it!

Here are some natural ways to incorporate pet names:

  • Greeting your partner: "Oi, amor!" (Hi, love!)
  • Asking for something: "Amor, pode me passar a água?" (Love, can you pass me the water?)
  • Expressing affection: "Te amo, meu bem." (I love you, darling.)
  • Comforting them: "Calma, amorzinho." (Calm down, sweetheart.)

The Gender Agreement Rule

Remember that many Portuguese pet names change based on whether you're speaking to a man or woman:

Masculine Feminine Meaning
Meu amor Minha amor My love
Querido Querida Dear
Lindo Linda Beautiful
Fofo Fofa Cute
Gatinho Gatinha Kitten

Note: "Amor" itself doesn't change (it's always masculine), but the possessive "meu/minha" might be used interchangeably colloquially.

Practice These Sweet Words

The best way to learn these pet names is to start using them! Try texting your partner "Bom dia, amor!" tomorrow morning, or whispering "Te amo, meu bem" before bed. The smile on their face will tell you everything you need to know about how much these words mean.

Portuguese pet names carry warmth, affection, and the beautiful musicality of the language. Each one is a small gift you can give your partner every day.

title: Portuguese Pronunciation: Key Sounds
content_html:
Good pronunciation is the key to being understood in Portuguese—and to understanding your partner when they speak naturally. This guide covers everything you need to know to sound more confident and authentic in Portuguese.

The Portuguese Alphabet: Getting Started

💕

Phrase to Learn

Como se pronuncia?

How do you pronounce it?

[ KOH-moo see proh-NOON-see-ah ]

Ask your partner this whenever you encounter a new word!

The Portuguese alphabet has 26 letters, the same as English. However, many letters are pronounced differently, and Portuguese has sounds that don't exist in English at all.

Vowels: The Heart of Portuguese

Portuguese vowels are more complex than English ones, especially because of nasal sounds.

Open Vowels

Amor Love

Pronunciation: ah-MOR

"Amor (love) - ah-MOR"

Café Coffee

Pronunciation: kah-FEH

"Café (coffee) - kah-FEH"

Avó Grandmother

Pronunciation: ah-VAW

"Avó (grandmother) - ah-VAW"

Closed Vowels

Mesa Table

Pronunciation: MAY-zah

"Mesa (table) - MAY-zah"

Avô Grandfather

Pronunciation: ah-VOH

"Avô (grandfather) - ah-VOH"

🇵🇹

Accent Marks Tell the Story

Portuguese accent marks aren't decorative—they tell you exactly how to pronounce a word. "Avó" (grandmother) has an open 'o', while "avô" (grandfather) has a closed 'o'. The marks are your pronunciation guides!

Nasal Vowels: The Portuguese Signature Sound

This is what makes Portuguese sound so distinctive. Nasal vowels are produced by letting air flow through both your mouth and nose.

Pão Bread

Pronunciation: POWNG

"Pão (bread) - POWNG"

Coração Heart

Pronunciation: koh-rah-SOWNG

"Coração (heart) - koh-rah-SOWNG"

Corações Hearts

Pronunciation: koh-rah-SOYSH

"Corações (hearts) - koh-rah-SOYSH"

Mãe Mother

Pronunciation: MAYNG

"Mãe (mother) - MAYNG"

How to Practice Nasal Sounds

  1. Say "song" and notice how the 'ng' resonates in your nose
  2. Now try to make that nasal quality on a vowel without the 'g' sound
  3. Practice with "pão" (bread)—start with "pow" then add nasality
🇵🇹

The Nasal Trick

Pinch your nose while saying a nasal vowel—you should feel vibration and resistance. If the sound comes out the same as without pinching, you're not nasalizing enough!

Consonants: Key Differences from English

The Letter R

Portuguese 'R' is famously tricky because it has multiple sounds:

Rua Street

Pronunciation: HOO-ah / RROO-ah

"Rua (street) - HOO-ah (Brazil), RROO-ah (Portugal)"

Caro Expensive

Pronunciation: KAH-roo

"Caro (expensive) - KAH-roo"

Amor Love

Pronunciation: ah-MOR / ah-MOH

"Amor (love) - ah-MOR or ah-MOH"

The Letter S

Sol Sun

Pronunciation: SOHL

"Sol (sun) - SOHL"

Casa House

Pronunciation: KAH-zah

"Casa (house) - KAH-zah"

Festa Party

Pronunciation: FESH-tah / FES-tah

"Festa (party) - FESH-tah (Rio)"

Letters That Change Sound

Dia Day

Pronunciation: JEE-ah

"Dia (day) - JEE-ah"

Noite Night

Pronunciation: NOY-chee

"Noite (night) - NOY-chee"

🇵🇹

Regional Variations

The D→J and T→CH changes are Brazilian. In Portugal, D and T keep their original sounds. Ask your partner which variety they speak so you can match their pronunciation!

Brazilian vs European Portuguese Pronunciation

The differences are significant enough that learners should be aware:

Feature Brazilian European
Vowels More open, clearer More reduced, "swallowed"
R sound 'H' sound (initial/double) Guttural or trilled
Unstressed E Sounds like 'ee' Often silent
De/Te "Jee/Chee" before 'i' "De/Te" (unchanged)
Overall tempo Slower, melodic Faster, compact

Brazilian Example

Muito obrigado Thank you very much

Pronunciation: MOON-too oh-bree-GAH-doo

"Muito obrigado pela sua ajuda. (Thank you very much for your help.)"

European Example

The Letters LH and NH

These combinations create sounds that don't exist as written in English:

Filho Son

Pronunciation: FEE-lyoo

"Filho (son) - FEE-lyoo"

Amanhã Tomorrow

Pronunciation: ah-mah-NYAH

"Amanhã (tomorrow) - ah-mah-NYAH"

Trabalho Work

Pronunciation: trah-BAH-lyoo

"O trabalho é difícil. (The work is difficult/learn/en/pt/is-portuguese-hard-to-learn/">difficult.)"

Minha My (feminine)

Pronunciation: MEE-nyah

"Minha casa é sua casa. (My house is your house.)"

Stress Patterns: Where to Put the Emphasis

Portuguese words follow predictable stress patterns:

  1. Words ending in -a, -e, -o, -am, -em: Stress the second-to-last syllable
  2. Words ending in -i, -u, or consonants: Stress the last syllable
  3. Words with accent marks: Stress falls on the accented syllable
Casa House

Pronunciation: KAH-zah

"A casa é grande. (The house is big.)"

Aqui Here

Pronunciation: ah-KEE

"Ele está aqui. (He is here.)"

Música Music

Pronunciation: MOO-zee-kah

"Eu gosto de ouvir música. (I like to listen to music.)"

Common Pronunciation Mistakes to Avoid

1. Pronouncing final E like English

Leite Milk

Pronunciation: LAY-chee

"Eu quero um copo de leite. (I want a glass of milk.)"

2. Using English R

Rio River / Rio (city)

Pronunciation: HEE-oo

"O Rio de Janeiro é lindo. (Rio de Janeiro is beautiful.)"

3. Forgetting nasalization

Sim Yes

Pronunciation: seeng

"Você vem? Sim! (Are you coming? Yes!)"

Practice Words for Couples

Beijo Kiss

Pronunciation: BAY-zhoo

"Me dá um beijo? (Give me a kiss?)"

Abraço Hug

Pronunciation: ah-BRAH-soo

"Vem cá, quero um abraço. (Come here, I want a hug.)"

Juntos Together

Pronunciation: ZHOON-toosh

"Sempre juntos. (Always together.)"

Saudade Longing/Missing

Pronunciation: sow-DAH-jee

"Que saudade de você! (How I miss you!)"

🇵🇹

Saudade: The Untranslatable Word

"Saudade" is considered one of the hardest words to translate. It's a deep emotional longing for someone or something you love but are separated from. Perfect pronunciation of this word will impress any Portuguese speaker!

Tips for Improving Your Pronunciation

  1. Record yourself and compare to native speakers
  2. Practice with your partner daily—even 5 minutes helps
  3. Listen to Portuguese music and sing along
  4. Watch videos with subtitles to connect sounds with spelling
  5. Use the shadowing technique: Listen and repeat immediately after native speakers

Start Speaking Today

Pronunciation improves with practice, and you have the best practice partner possible—someone who loves you and wants to help you succeed. Don't wait until your pronunciation is "perfect." Start speaking Portuguese today, and let your partner gently guide you toward better sounds.

Every word you pronounce correctly is a step closer to their heart.

title: Portuguese Proposal & Engagement Phrases: How to Pop the Question
content_html:
Proposing in your partner's native language is one of the most romantic gestures you can make. Because Portuguese is a Romance language, it possesses a melodic quality that lends itself perfectly to declarations of devotion. Whether you're planning an elaborate pedido de casamento or a simple, heartfelt moment, knowing how to express your intentions in Portuguese will make this milestone unforgettable. This guide covers everything from the perfect proposal phrases to announcing your noivado to family and friends.

Using the local language shows a deep respect for your partner's heritage and identity. It demonstrates that you are willing to put in the effort to communicate in the way that feels most natural to them, even during your most nervous moments. For more romantic expressionst/portuguese-love-letter-phrases-for-english-speakers/">romantic expressionst/portuguese-flirting-phrases-for-english-speakers/">romantic expressions, see our guide on Portuguese pet names and terms of endearment.

💕

Phrase to Learn

Quer casar comigo?

Will you marry me?

[ KEHR kah-ZAHR koom-EE-goo? ]

The most important question you'll ever ask.

The Proposal Vocabularylearn/en/pt/portuguese-moving-in-together/">Vocabularylearn/en/pt/portuguese-honeymoon-travel-phrases/">Vocabularylearn/en/pt/portuguese-food-and-restaurant-vocabulary/">Vocabularylearn/en/pt/portuguese-baby-pregnancy-vocabulary/">Vocabularylearn/en/pt/portuguese-100-common-phrases-for-english-speakers/">Vocabulary

Master these essential words before your big moment:

anel ring

Pronunciation: ah-NEHL

"O anel de noivado brilha lindamente."

Portuguese English Context
pedido de casamento proposal The act of proposing
noivado engagement The period before marriage
anel ring The engagement ring
noivo fiancé Engaged man
noiva fiancée Engaged woman
sim yes The answer you're hoping for
para sempre forever For eternity
amor da minha vida love of my life Romantic declaration

In Portuguese, nouns are gender-specific. If you are speaking to a man, you would refer to him as your noivo, whereas a woman is your noiva. This distinction is important when you begin introducing your partner to others after the proposal has taken place.

🇵🇹

Proposal Traditions

In Portuguese-speaking cultures, proposal traditions may include asking the parents for their blessing beforehand. This traditional gesture is still valued by many families and can make a wonderful impression.

Ways to Propose

Every couple has a unique rhythm, and your proposal should reflect the specific bond you share. In many Portuguese-speaking cultures, the pedido de casamento is viewed as a bridge between two families, making the choice of words particularly significant. Whether you are in a crowded restaurant or a secluded beach, the phrasing sets the emotional stage for your future together.

Choosing the right words involves matching the intensity of your relationship with the setting you have chosen. While the ultimate goal is the same, the linguistic path you take can vary from a traditional question to a poetic declaration of future intentions. You might prefer a short, punchy sentence or a longer speech that details your journey as a couple.

The following subsections will explore the standard way to ask for someone's hand in marriage, as well as more elaborate variations that incorporate terms of endearment. These options allow you to tailor your speech to be either classic or deeply sentimental, ensuring the amor da minha vida feels the weight of the moment.

promessa promise

Pronunciation: proh-MEH-ssah

"Um casamento é uma promessa solene."

The Classic Proposal

"Quer casar comigo?" - Will you marry me?

This simple, direct question is perfect for any setting. Practice the pronunciation until it feels natural.

More Romantic Variations

  • "You are the amor da minha vida. Quer casar comigo?" - Adding a declaration of love
  • "I want to spend para sempre with you." - Building to the question
  • "Will you make me the happiest person in the world?" - An alternative approach
noivo/noiva engaged

Pronunciation: NOY-voo / NOY-vah

"Estamos noivos!"

Announcing Your Engagement

Once your partner says "sim!", you'll want to share the news. In Lusophone cultures, family is often central to life, so the announcement is a major event. You might find yourself repeating these phrases many times over as you call relatives and friends to spread the joy of your noivado.

To Family

  • "We're noivo/noiva!" - The joyful announcement
  • "noivo/noiva said sim!" - Sharing the excitement
  • "We have wonderful news!" - Building anticipation

To Friends

  • "Guess what happened?" - Creating suspense
  • "Look at my anel!" - Showing off the ring
  • "We're getting married!" - Direct and joyful

On Social Media

  • "She/He said sim!" - Classic announcement
  • "para sempre starts now!" - Romantic caption
  • "noivo/noiva to my amor da minha vida" - Heartfelt declaration
🇵🇹

Engagement Celebrations

In many Portuguese-speaking families, an noivado party is held to celebrate the couple. This gathering allows both families to meet and celebrate the upcoming union together.

Ring Vocabulary

The anel is central to the pedido de casamento. In many Portuguese-speaking regions, it is common for the couple to wear plain gold bands on their right hands during the engagement, moving them to the left hand after the wedding ceremony.

Portuguese English Description
anel ring The piece of jewelry
Engagement anel engagement ring The symbol of commitment
Diamond diamond Popular gemstone
Gold gold Common metal
Fit fit How the ring sits

Preparing Your Proposal

Delivering a pedido de casamento in a second language requires a bit more than just memorizing a single sentence. You want the words to flow naturally so that the emotion of the moment is the primary focus. Preparation involves focusing on the rhythm of the Portuguese language and ensuring your pronunciation of key terms like noivado and casar is clear.

Beyond the linguistic hurdles, a successful proposal requires a blend of logistical planning and emotional readiness. Portuguese pronunciation, with its specific stress patterns and nasal sounds, can be tricky when heart rates are high. Focusing on the cadence of your sentences beforehand helps ensure that your partner understands every word of your request without confusion.

Below, we look at "Practice Tips" specifically tailored for language learners to help build muscle memory. We also explore "Setting the Scene," which involves integrating Portuguese cultural elements or personal milestones into the physical environment to create a cohesive and memorable experience for both of you.

surpresa surprise

Pronunciation: soor-PREH-zah

"O pedido foi uma surpresa total."

Practice Tips

  1. Record yourself saying the proposal
  2. Practice with your partner's name
  3. Time your delivery for natural flow
  4. Prepare for emotion - it's okay to be nervous

Setting the Scene

Consider these romantic elements:

  • A meaningful location
  • The right moment
  • Having someone to photograph (secretly!)
  • The anel ready and accessible

After They Say Yes

The moments after the pedido de casamento are magical. This is the time to switch from the formal question to more relaxed, affectionate language as you begin your life as a noivo or noiva.

Moment What to Say Translation
Right after I love you so much! Declaration of love
First call We're noivo/noiva! Announcement
First photo My noivo/noiva! New title

For more ways to express your love, explore our how to say I love you in Portuguese guide.

Quick Reference

Purpose Phrase
Propose Quer casar comigo?
React to yes You said sim!
Announce We're noivo/noiva!
Introduce My noivo/noiva

Your pedido de casamento in Portuguese will be a story you'll tell para sempre. Take your time, speak from the heart, and let your love shine through every word.

title: 50 Romantic Portuguese Phrases for Every Occasion to Melt Your Partner's Heart
content_html:
There's something magical about whispering sweet words in Portuguese to your loved one. Whether you're starting your day together or ending it in each other's arms, these romantic phrasesen/pt/portuguese-flirting-phrases-for-english-speakers/">romantic phrases will deepen your connection and bring you closer. From tender morning greetings to passionate declarations, let's explore the most beautiful ways to express your love in Portuguese.

💕

Phrase to Learn

Amor da minha vida

Love of my life

[ ah-MOHR dah MEE-nyah VEE-dah ]

The ultimate romantic phrase - use it during intimate moments or when expressing deep commitment.

Essential Love Declarations (1-10)

These are the phrases that will make your partner's heart skip a beat. Practice them together and watch your connection grow stronger!

Eu te amo I love you

Pronunciation: eh-oo chee AH-moo

"Eu te amo mais cada dia que passa."

Portuguese English Pronunciation When to Use
Eu te amo I love you eh-oo chee AH-moh Daily expressions of love
Eu te adoro I adore you eh-oo chee ah-DOH-roo Sweet, tender moments
Você é tudo para mim You're everything to me voh-SEH eh TOO-doh PAH-rah meeng Deep emotional conversations
Sou apaixonado por você I'm in love with you soh ah-pah-ee-shoh-NAH-doh por voh-SEH Confessing your feelings
Meu coração é seu My heart is yours meh-oo koh-rah-SAH-ooh eh SEH-oo Romantic declarations
Você me completa You complete me voh-SEH mee kohm-PLEH-tah When feeling grateful
Estou louco por você I'm crazy about you eh-STOH LOH-koh por voh-SEH Passionate moments
Você é meu mundo You're my world voh-SEH eh meh-oo MOON-doh Expressing deep love
Não posso viver sem você I can't live without you nah-oh POH-soh vee-VEHR sayng voh-SEH Intense romantic moments
És o amor da minha vida You're the love of my life ehs ooh ah-MOHR dah MEE-nyah VEE-dah Life-changing declarations

Sweet Daily Expressions (11-20)

These everyday phrases will keep the romance alive in your daily routine together:

  • Bom dia, meu amor (Good morning, my love) - "bohm DEE-ah, meh-oo ah-MOHR"
  • Boa noite, querida (Good night, darling) - "BOH-ah NOY-chee, keh-REE-dah"
  • Durma bem, amor (Sleep well, love) - "DOOR-mah bayng, ah-MOHR"
  • Sonhe comigo (Dream of me) - "soh-NYEH koh-MEE-goh"
  • Saudades de você (I miss you) - "sah-OO-dah-jees djee voh-SEH"
  • Pensei em você (I was thinking of you) - "payn-SAY ahng voh-SEH"
  • Que saudade! (I miss you so much!) - "kee sah-OO-dah-djee"
  • Estou com saudades (I'm missing you) - "eh-STOH kohng sah-OO-dah-jees"
🇵🇹

Brazilian vs European Portuguese

Notice the pronunciation differences between Brazilian and European Portuguese. In Brazil, "de" sounds like "jee" while in Portugal it's more like "duh". Want to learn more? Check out our guide on Brazilian vs European Portuguese differences.

Compliments and Adoration (21-30)

Make your partner feel special with these beautiful compliments:

Você é lindo/a You're beautiful/handsome

Pronunciation: voh-SEH eh LEEN-doh/dah

"Você é linda quando sorri."

Portuguese English Pronunciation Perfect Moment
Você é lindo/a You're beautiful/handsome voh-SEH eh LEEN-doh/dah When they look amazing
Você é incrível You're incredible voh-SEH eh een-kree-VEH-ooh Praising their qualities
Adoro seu sorriso I love your smile ah-DOH-roo SEH-oo soh-REE-zoh When they smile at you
Você é perfeito/a You're perfect voh-SEH eh pehr-FAY-toh/tah During romantic moments
Seus olhos são lindos Your eyes are beautiful says OH-lyohs sah-ooh LEEN-dohs Deep eye contact moments
Você é especial You're special voh-SEH eh eh-speh-see-AHL Expressing uniqueness
Adoro estar com você I love being with you ah-DOH-roo eh-STAH kohng voh-SEH Quality time together
Você me faz feliz You make me happy voh-SEH meh fahz feh-LEEZ Expressing gratitude
És maravilhoso/a You're wonderful ehs mah-rah-veel-YOH-zoh/zah Appreciating them
Você é meu sonho You're my dream voh-SEH eh meh-oo SOH-nyoh Ultimate compliment

Intimate and Passionate Phrases (31-40)

For those moments when words need to convey deeper passion and intimacy:

  • Quero te beijar (I want to kiss you) - "KEH-roh chee bay-ZHAR"
  • Me abraça (Hug me) - "mee ah-BRAH-sah"
  • Preciso de você (I need you) - "preh-SEE-zoh djee voh-SEH"
  • Você me enlouquece (You drive me crazy) - "voh-SEH mee ayng-loh-KEH-seh"
  • Quero estar sempre com você (I want to be with you always) - "KEH-roh eh-STAH SEM-preh kohng voh-SEH"
  • Você é meu desejo (You're my desire) - "voh-SEH eh meh-oo deh-ZAY-zhoh"
  • Meu coração bate por você (My heart beats for you) - "meh-oo koh-rah-SAH-ooh BAH-teh por voh-SEH"
  • Estou completamente apaixonado (I'm completely in love) - "eh-STOH kohm-pleh-tah-MAYN-teh ah-pah-ee-shoh-NAH-doh"
  • Você é minha alma gêmea (You're my soulmate) - "voh-SEH eh MEE-nyah AHL-mah ZHEH-mee-ah"
  • Nosso amor é eterno (Our love is eternal) - "NOH-soh ah-MOHR eh eh-TEHR-noh"

Future and Commitment Phrases (41-50)

Express your hopes and dreams for your future together:

Quero envelhecer com você I want to grow old with you

Pronunciation: KEH-roh ayng-veh-lyeh-SEHR kohng voh-SEH

"Quero envelhecer com você e construir uma vida juntos."

Portuguese English Pronunciation For Special Moments
Quero envelhecer com você I want to grow old with you KEH-roh ayng-veh-lyeh-SEHR kohng voh-SEH Talking about forever
Vamos construir juntos Let's build together VAH-mohs kohng-STROO-eer ZHOON-tohs Planning your future
Você é meu futuro You're my future voh-SEH eh meh-oo foo-TOO-roh Serious commitments
Quero passar o resto da vida com você I want to spend the rest of my life with you KEH-roh pah-SAH ooh HEH-stoh dah VEE-dah kohng voh-SEH Life-changing moments
Nosso amor será sempre forte Our love will always be strong NOH-soh ah-MOHR seh-RAH SEM-preh FOR-teh Overcoming challenges
Com você, tudo é possível With you, everything is possible kohng voh-SEH, TOO-doh eh poh-SEE-vehl Supporting dreams
Você faz meu mundo melhor You make my world better voh-SEH fahz meh-oo MOON-doh mehl-HOHR Daily appreciation
Sou grato por ter você na minha vida I'm grateful to have you in my life soh GRAH-toh por tehr voh-SEH nah MEE-nyah VEE-dah Expressing gratitude
Você é minha paz You're my peace voh-SEH eh MEE-nyah pahz Comforting moments
Amo cada momento com você I love every moment with you AH-moh KAH-dah moh-MAYN-toh kohng voh-SEH Cherishing time together

How to Use These Romantic Phrases

Ready to put these phrases into practice? Here are some tips for using them naturally:

Start small: Begin with simpler phrases like "Bom dia, meu amor" and gradually work up to more complex expressions. Practice them during your daily Portuguese greetings and farewells routine.

Match the moment: Choose phrases that fit the situation. "Estou com saudades" works perfectly when you're apart, while "Você me completa" is ideal during deep conversations.

Use authentic pronunciation: The phonetic guides help you sound natural. Remember that Portuguese has nasal sounds - practice makes perfect!

Be genuine: These phrases carry emotional weight. Use them when you truly feel the sentiment to maintain their special meaning.

🇵🇹

Timing is Everything

Portuguese speakers often use terms of endearment more frequently than English speakers. Don't be surprised if your partner responds with even sweeter expressions! For more Portuguese pet names, explore our dedicated guide.

Making It Personal

The most romantic thing you can do is adapt these phrases to your unique relationship. Add your partner's name, reference shared memories, or create your own variations. Remember, language learning is about connection, not perfection.

Ready to dive deeper into romantic Portuguese? Explore our complete guide to how to say I love you in Portuguese for even more heartfelt expressions.

title: Portuguese Shopping Vocabulary
content_html:
Shopping is a universal activity that becomes an adventure when visiting Portuguese-speaking countries with your partner. Whether browsing markets in Brazil or shops in Portugal, this vocabularylearn/en/pt/portuguese-weather-vocabulary/">vocabularylearn/en/pt/portuguese-moving-in-together/">vocabularylearn/en/pt/portuguese-honeymoon-travel-phrases/">vocabularylearn/en/pt/portuguese-food-and-restaurant-vocabulary/">vocabularylearn/en/pt/portuguese-baby-pregnancy-vocabulary/">vocabularylearn/en/pt/portuguese-100-common-phrases-for-english-speakers/">vocabulary will help you navigate any retail experience confidently.

Starting a Shopping Interaction

💕

Phrase to Learn

Posso ajudar?

Can I help you?

[ POH-soo ah-zhoo-DAHR ]

What store employees typically ask when you enter. Be prepared to respond!

Estou só olhando I am just looking

Pronunciation: esh-TOH soh oh-LYAHN-doo

"Obrigado, estou só olhando por enquanto. (Thanks, I am just looking for now.)"

Estou procurando... I am looking for...

Pronunciation: esh-TOH proh-koo-RAHN-doo

"Estou procurando um presente para minha esposa. (I am looking for a gift for my wife.)"

🇧🇷

Greeting Shop Staff

In Portuguese-speaking countries, it is polite to greet shop staff when entering. A simple "Bom dia" or "Boa tarde" shows respect and often leads to better service.

Asking About Products

Quanto custa isso? How much does this cost?

Pronunciation: KWAN-too KOOS-tah EE-soo

"Com licença, quanto custa isso? (Excuse me, how much does this cost?)"

Qual é o preço? What is the price?

Pronunciation: kwahl eh oo PREH-soo

"Gostei deste. Qual é o preço? (I like this one. What is the price?)"

Tem desconto? Is there a discount?

Pronunciation: tayn dehs-KON-too

"Se eu comprar dois, tem desconto? (If I buy two, is there a discount?)"

Aceita cartão? Do you accept cards?

Pronunciation: ah-SEH-ee-tah kahr-TOWN

"Aceita cartão de crédito? (Do you accept credit cards?)"

Sizes and Fitting

Que tamanho você usa? What size do you wear?

Pronunciation: kee tah-MAH-nyoo voh-SEH OO-zah

"Amor, que tamanho você usa? (Love, what size do you wear?)"

Tem em outro tamanho? Do you have it in another size?

Pronunciation: tayn ayn OH-troo tah-MAH-nyoo

"Este está pequeno. Tem em outro tamanho? (This one is small. Do you have it in another size?)"

Posso experimentar? Can I try it on?

Pronunciation: POH-soo esh-peh-ree-men-TAHR

"Gostei dessa blusa. Posso experimentar? (I like this blouse. Can I try it on?)"

Onde fica o provador? Where is the fitting room?

Pronunciation: ON-jee FEE-kah oo proh-vah-DOR

"Onde fica o provador, por favor? (Where is the fitting room, please?)"

Common Clothing Items

🇵🇹

Brazilian vs Portuguese Terms

Some clothing terms differ between Brazil and Portugal. For example, "calcinha" (panties) in Brazil is "cuecas" in Portugal for women's underwear. Be aware of regional vocabulary when shopping.

Camisa / Camiseta Shirt / T-shirt

Pronunciation: kah-MEE-zah / kah-mee-ZEH-tah

"Quero comprar uma camisa para o meu namorado. (I want to buy a shirt for my boyfriend.)"

Calça / Calças Pants / Trousers

Pronunciation: KAW-sah / KAW-sahz

"Essas calças ficaram perfeitas em você. (Those pants look perfect on you.)"

Vestido Dress

Pronunciation: vehs-CHEE-doo

"Que vestido lindo! Você quer experimentar? (What a beautiful dress! Do you want to try it on?)"

Sapatos Shoes

Pronunciation: sah-PAH-toosh

"Preciso de sapatos novos para o casamento. (I need new shoes for the wedding.)"

Colors

Preto, branco, azul Black, white, blue

Pronunciation: PREH-too, BRAHN-koo, ah-ZOOL

"Tem essa camisa em preto? (Do you have this shirt in black?)"

Vermelho, verde, amarelo Red, green, yellow

Pronunciation: vehr-MEH-lyoo, VEHR-dee, ah-mah-REH-loo

"Prefiro a cor vermelha. (I prefer the red color.)"

Rosa, roxo, laranja Pink, purple, orange

Pronunciation: HOH-zah, HOH-shoo, lah-RAHN-zhah

"O roxo combina com seus olhos. (Purple matches your eyes.)"

Making Decisions

Vou levar este I will take this one

Pronunciation: voh leh-VAHR EHS-chee

"Perfeito. Vou levar este. (Perfect. I will take this one.)"

Preciso pensar I need to think about it

Pronunciation: preh-SEE-zoo pen-SAHR

"É bonito, mas preciso pensar. (It is nice, but I need to think about it.)"

Não é bem o que eu queria It is not quite what I wanted

Pronunciation: now eh bayn oo kee EH-oo keh-REE-ah

"Obrigado, mas não é bem o que eu queria. (Thank you, but it is not quite what I wanted.)"

At the Checkout

Onde fica o caixa? Where is the checkout?

Pronunciation: ON-jee FEE-kah oo KAH-ee-shah

"Já escolhi. Onde fica o caixa? (I have chosen. Where is the checkout?)"

Quer embrulhar para presente? Would you like it gift-wrapped?

Pronunciation: kehr em-broo-LYAHR PAH-rah preh-ZEN-chee

"Sim, por favor. É um presente. (Yes, please. It is a gift.)"

Pode colocar numa sacola? Can you put it in a bag?

Pronunciation: POH-jee koh-loh-KAHR NOO-mah sah-KOH-lah

"Pode colocar tudo numa sacola só? (Can you put everything in one bag?)"

Shopping Together as a Couple

O que você acha? What do you think?

Pronunciation: oo kee voh-SEH AH-shah

"Amor, o que você acha deste? (Love, what do you think of this one?)"

Fica lindo em você It looks beautiful on you

Pronunciation: FEE-kah LEEN-doo ayn voh-SEH

"Experimenta. Tenho certeza que fica lindo em você. (Try it on. I am sure it looks beautiful on you.)"

Vamos comprar para os dois Let us buy for both of us

Pronunciation: VAH-mohs kom-PRAHR PAH-rah oosh doysh

"Que tal? Vamos comprar para os dois? (How about it? Let us buy for both of us?)"

Shopping with your partner becomes even more fun when you can communicate in Portuguese. Practice these phrases before your next shopping adventure together.

title: Portuguese Small Talk: Conversation Starters for Every Situation
content_html:
Small talk is the foundation of every relationship. Whether you're meeting your partner's friends, familyf="/learn/en/pt/meeting-your-portuguese-partners-family/">family, or just chatting with new people, these Portuguese conversation starters will help you connect naturally.

💕

Phrase to Learn

Bom dia!

Good morning!

[ bohm DEE-ah! ]

The most commonearn/en/pt/portuguese-moving-in-together/">most commonearn/en/pt/portuguese-honeymoon-travel-phrases/">most commonearn/en/pt/portuguese-food-and-restaurant-vocabulary/">most commonearn/en/pt/portuguese-baby-pregnancy-vocabulary/">most commonearn/en/pt/portuguese-100-common-phrases-for-english-speakers/">most common way to apologize

Essential Phrases

These phrases are essential for make conversation with your Portuguese-speaking partner:

Portuguese English Pronunciation
Desculpa I'm sorry desh-KOOL-pah

Desculpa (desh-KOOL-pah) is the most important phrase to know. Use it whenever you need to make conversation.

🇵🇹

Cultural Tip

In Portuguese culture, small talk often touches on family and personal wellbeing before moving to other topics. Showing genuine interest in someone's family is appreciated.

More Useful Expressions

Portuguese English Pronunciation
Me perdoa Forgive me mee per-DOH-ah
A culpa é minha It's my fault ah KOOL-pah eh MEEN-yah
Sinto muito I'm so sorry SEEN-too MWEEN-too
Não vai acontecer de novo It won't happen again nowng vai ah-kon-teh-SER jee NOH-voo
Eu entendo como você se sente I understand how you feel eh-oo en-TEN-doo KOH-moo voh-SEH seh SEN-chee
Tudo bem? How are you? / Is everything okay?

Pronunciation: TOO-doo BENG?

"Olá! Tudo bem com você?"

Additional Phrases

Portuguese English Pronunciation
Quero compensar I want to make it up to you KEH-roo kom-pen-SAR
Perdão Sorry / Pardon per-DOWNG
Eu cometi um erro I made a mistake eh-oo koh-meh-TEE oom EH-roo

Beyond Apologies: Breaking the Ice

While knowing how to apologize is vital, true small talk involves opening doors to new topics. In Portuguese-speaking environments, transitions are often fluid. Start with a greeting and quickly move into a "check-in" question. Using "Tudo bem?" is the standard, but you can be more specific to show engagement.

Portuguese English Pronunciation
Como vai? How is it going? KOH-moo VAY?
Como foi o seu dia? How was your day? KOH-moo FOY oo SEH-oo JEE-ah?
O que você conta de novo? What’s new? oo KEH voh-SEH KOHN-tah jee NOH-voo?
Como foi o seu dia? How was your day?

Pronunciation: KOH-moo FOY oo SEW DEE-ah?

"Oi, como foi o seu dia no trabalho?"

Talking About Your Environment

When you find yourself in a lull, the weather or the immediate surroundings provide a safe harbor for conversation. In Brazil and Portugal, commenting on the heat or the beauty of a location is a standard social lubricant.

Portuguese English Pronunciation
Está calor hoje, não é? It’s hot today, isn’t it? es-TAH kah-LOR OH-jee, nowng EH?
Que lugar bonito! What a beautiful place! KEH loo-GAR boo-NEE-too!
Você vem sempre aqui? Do you come here often? voh-SEH VENG SEM-pree ah-KEE?
O tempo está bom hoje, não é? The weather is good today, isn't it?

Pronunciation: oo TEM-poo ehs-TAH bohm OH-jeh, nah-oong EH?

"Que maravilha! O tempo está bom hoje, não é?"

Finding Common Ground

Asking about hobbies or work helps build a deeper connection. Focus on open-ended questions that allow the other person to share details about their life.

Portuguese English Pronunciation
O que você gosta de fazer? What do you like to do? oo KEH voh-SEH GOSH-tah jee fah-ZER?
Você trabalha com o quê? What do you do for work? voh-SEH trah-BAHL-yah KOM oo KEH?
Você gosta dessa música? Do you like this music? voh-SEH GOSH-tah DEH-sah MOO-zee-kah?
Você tem planos para o fim de semana? Do you have plans for the weekend?

Pronunciation: voh-SEH teng PLAH-noos PAH-rah oo feeng jee seh-MAH-nah?

"Depois de uma semana, você tem planos para o fim de semana?"

Ending the Conversation Gracefully

Knowing how to exit a conversation is just as important as starting one. In Portuguese, it is polite to express that you enjoyed the interaction before leaving.

Portuguese English Pronunciation
Foi um prazer falar com você It was a pleasure talking to you FOY oom prah-ZER fah-LAR KOM voh-SEH
Tenha um bom dia Have a good day TEN-yah oom BOHNG JEE-ah
A gente se vê! See you! (lit. 'We see each other') ah JEN-chee seh VEH!

Tips for Using These Phrases

  1. Start simple - Basic phrasesrn/en/pt/essential-portuguese-phrases-for-couples/">Basic phrases are perfectly fine
  2. Ask follow-up questions - Shows genuine interest
  3. Listen actively - Respond to what they share
  4. Don't fear mistakes - People appreciate the effort
  5. Smile and nod - Universal communication helpers

Practice Makes Perfect

Learning to make conversation in your partner's language shows dedication and care. Even if your pronunciation isn't perfect, the effort itself demonstrates your commitment to the relationship.

Related Articles

  • Essential Portuguese Phrases for Couples
  • How to Say I Love You in Portuguese
  • Portuguese Pet Names and Terms of Endearment

title: Portuguese Subjunctive Mood Explained
content_html:
The subjunctive mood is one of the most challengingearn/en/pt/is-portuguese-hard-to-learn/">challenging aspects of Portuguese grammar="/learn/en/pt/portuguese-grammar-basics-for-beginners/">grammar for English speakers. While English rarely uses the subjunctive, Portuguese employs it constantly. Mastering this mood will significantly elevate your Portuguese fluency and help you express nuanced emotions with your partner.

What Is the Subjunctive?

💕

Phrase to Learn

Espero que você esteja bem

I hope that you are well

[ esh-PEH-roo kee voh-SEH esh-TEH-zhah beyn ]

The word 'esteja' is the subjunctive form of 'estar' - used after expressions of hope

The subjunctive expresses uncertainty, emotion, desire, or hypothetical situations. Unlike the indicative mood which states facts, the subjunctive deals with the realm of possibility and feeling.

🇧🇷

Why the Subjunctive Matters

Using the subjunctive correctly marks you as a more advanced speaker. Native speakers notice when learners master this mood because it shows deeper understanding of how Portuguese expresses emotion and uncertainty.

Present Subjunctive Formation

falar (subjunctive)

to speak

que eu fale
que você/ele/ela fale
que nós falemos
que vocês/eles/elas falem

comer (subjunctive)

to eat

que eu coma
que você/ele/ela coma
que nós comamos
que vocês/eles/elas comam

partir (subjunctive)

to leave

que eu parta
que você/ele/ela parta
que nós partamos
que vocês/eles/elas partam

Essential Irregular Subjunctives

ser (subjunctive)

to be

que eu seja
que você/ele/ela seja
que nós sejamos
que vocês/eles/elas sejam

estar (subjunctive)

to be

que eu esteja
que você/ele/ela esteja
que nós estejamos
que vocês/eles/elas estejam

ter (subjunctive)

to have

que eu tenha
que você/ele/ela tenha
que nós tenhamos
que vocês/eles/elas tenham

When to Use the Subjunctive

English speakers often struggle with the Portuguese subjunctive because English has almost entirely lost its own subjunctive mood. We have fossilized traces — "If I were you," "God save the Queen" — but they feel like quirky exceptions, not a living grammatical system. In Portuguese, the subjunctive is everywhere. It appears in everyday sentences that you'd never think twice about in English, like Espero que estejas bem (I hope you're well). That estejas is subjunctive, triggered automatically by espero que — and using the indicative estás here would sound jarringly wrong to a native speaker.

The core logic is actually intuitive once you stop thinking in English terms: the subjunctive shows up when the outcome is filtered through someone's emotions, desires, or uncertainty rather than being stated as fact. Compare Sei que ela fala português (I know she speaks Portuguese — indicative, because it's a known fact) with Duvido que ela fale português (I doubt she speaks Portuguese — subjunctive, because doubt makes it uncertain). The verb falar changes from fala to fale solely because of that shift from certainty to doubt.

One pattern that trips up learners: the subjunctive trigger almost always sits in the main clause, while the subjunctive verb itself lives in the subordinate clause after que. So you need to train your ear to recognize trigger words and expressions — quero que, é importante que, embora, antes que — because the moment you hear one, you know the subjunctive is coming next.

After Expressions of Emotion

Fico feliz que você esteja aqui I am happy that you are here

Pronunciation: FEE-koo feh-LEEZ kee voh-SEH esh-TEH-zhah ah-KEE

"É importante que você esteja aqui agora."

Tenho medo que ele não venha I am afraid that he will not come

Pronunciation: TEH-nyoo MEH-doo kee EH-lee now VEH-nyah

"Ela teme que chova amanhã."

After Expressions of Desire or Wish

Quero que você seja feliz I want you to be happy

Pronunciation: KEH-roo kee voh-SEH SEH-zhah feh-LEEZ

"Desejo que tudo corra bem para você."

Espero que tudo corra bem I hope everything goes well

Pronunciation: esh-PEH-roo kee TOO-doo KOH-hah beyn

"É provável que ele chegue atrasado."

After Expressions of Doubt or Uncertainty

🇵🇹

Doubt vs Certainty

When you express certainty, use the indicative. When you express doubt, use the subjunctive. This distinction is crucial in Portuguese.

Duvido que isso seja verdade I doubt that this is true

Pronunciation: doo-VEE-doo kee EE-soo SEH-zhah vehr-DAH-dee

"Não acredito que ela saiba a resposta."

After Certain Conjunctions

Embora eu esteja cansado Although I am tired

Pronunciation: em-BOH-rah EH-oo esh-TEH-zhah kahn-SAH-doo

"Vou sair, embora esteja chovendo."

Vou esperar até que você chegue I will wait until you arrive

Pronunciation: voh esh-peh-RAHR ah-TEH kee voh-SEH SHEH-gee

"Não comece a festa até que eu volte."

Romantic Uses of the Subjunctive

Desejo que sejamos felizes para sempre I wish that we will be happy forever

Pronunciation: deh-ZEH-zhoo kee seh-ZHAH-mohs feh-LEE-zehs PAH-rah SEM-pree

"Espero que possamos viajar juntos."

Espero que você saiba quanto te amo I hope you know how much I love you

Pronunciation: esh-PEH-roo kee voh-SEH SAH-ee-bah KWAHN-too chee AH-moo

"É essencial que você saiba a verdade."

Não importa o que aconteça, estarei aqui No matter what happens, I will be here

Pronunciation: now eem-POR-tah oo kee ah-kon-TEH-sah, esh-tah-REH-ee ah-KEE

"Seja o que for que ele diga, não acredite."

Practice Tips

Start by recognizing when native speakers use the subjunctive. Listen for it in songs, movies, and conversations with your partner. Once you can identify it, begin incorporating it into your speech with common expressions like "Espero que..." (I hope that...) and "Quero que..." (I want that...).

The subjunctive takes time to master, but it adds beautiful nuance to your Portuguese. Your partner will appreciate your efforts to express emotions and hopes with grammatical precision.

title: Portuguese Emotional Support Phrases: Comfort Your Partner in Difficult Times
content_html:
When your Portuguese-speaking partner faces difficult times, offering comfort in their native language creates a deeper emotional bond. Portuguese warmth makes it perfect for expressing heartfelt support.

Phrases of Presence

Estou aqui para você I'm here for you

Você não está sozinho/sozinha nisso You're not alone in this

Pode sempre contar comigo You can always count on me

Expressing Empathy

Eu entendo o que você está passando I understand what you're going through

É normal se sentir assim It's normal to feel this way

Seus sentimentos são válidos Your feelings are valid

Offering Comfort

Vai ficar tudo bem Everything will be okay

Juntos podemos superar isso Together we can overcome this

Tenho muito orgulho de você I'm very proud of you

Active Support

O que posso fazer para ajudar? What can I do to help?

Quer conversar sobre isso ou prefere ficarmos juntos em silêncio? Do you want to talk about it or prefer we stay together quietly?

Me diz do que você precisa Tell me what you need

Specific Situations

Navigating emotional distress in a second language requires a keen understanding of context, as the words used to comfort a grieving partner differ significantly from those meant to soothe work-related anxiety. In Portuguese, the gravity of the situation dictates the choice of verbs and the level of formality involved in the delivery. This section examines the specific linguistic nuances needed to address three common life hurdles: grief, stress, and disappointment.

Effective communication during these times hinges on selecting phrases that match the intensity of the partner’s experience. While grief often calls for more traditional, somber expressions of solidarity, stress requires language focused on partnership and shared responsibility. Disappointment, on the other hand, is best met with vocabularylearn/en/pt/portuguese-shopping-vocabulary/">vocabularylearn/en/pt/portuguese-moving-in-together/">vocabularylearn/en/pt/portuguese-honeymoon-travel-phrases/">vocabularylearn/en/pt/portuguese-food-and-restaurant-vocabulary/">vocabularylearn/en/pt/portuguese-baby-pregnancy-vocabulary/">vocabularylearn/en/pt/portuguese-100-common-phrases-for-english-speakers/">vocabulary that validates the person's feelings while acknowledging the external circumstances. Understanding these distinctions prevents support from sounding generic or misplaced.

Acolhimento Welcoming / Providing shelter or support

Pronunciation: ah-koh-lyee-men-toh

"O acolhimento que você me deu durante esse tempo difícil significou muito para mim."

Grief

Sinto muito pela sua perda I'm so sorryef="/learn/en/pt/portuguese-forgive-phrases-for-english-speakers/">sorry for your loss

Stress

Respira comigo Breathe with me

Vamos passo a passo Let's go step by step

Disappointment

Sei o quanto isso era importante para você I know how important this was to you

Você não precisa ser forte o tempo todo You don't have to be strong all the time

Physical Comfort

Vem cá, deixa eu te abraçar Come here, let me hug you

Quer um abraço? Do you want a hug?

Encouragement

Você é mais forte do que pensa You're stronger than you think

Eu acredito em você I believe in you

Being Present

Você não precisa dizer nada You don't need to say anything

Só quero estar aqui com você I just want to be here with you

Portuguese warmth in support creates profound comfort and connection.

title: Portuguese Texting Slang for Couples
content_html:
Texting your Portuguese-speaking partner can feel like learning a whole new language within a language. Brazilian and Portuguese texters use countless abbreviations and slang terms. Understanding these will help you connect more naturally through messages and social media.

Essential Texting Abbreviations

💕

Phrase to Learn

Td bem?

Everything okay? / How are you?

[ too-doo beyn ]

Short for 'Tudo bem?' - the most commonearn/en/pt/portuguese-weather-vocabulary/">most commonearn/en/pt/portuguese-shopping-vocabulary/">most commonearn/en/pt/portuguese-moving-in-together/">most commonearn/en/pt/portuguese-honeymoon-travel-phrases/">most commonearn/en/pt/portuguese-food-and-restaurant-vocabulary/">most commonearn/en/pt/portuguese-baby-pregnancy-vocabulary/">most commonearn/en/pt/portuguese-100-common-phrases-for-english-speakers/">most common greeting in text messages

🇧🇷

Texting Culture Differences

Brazilians are known for being extremely active texters. It is common to exchange dozens of WhatsApp messages daily with a partner. Quick responses are expected and appreciated.

Vc You

Pronunciation: voh-SEH

"Vc está onde? (Where are you?) Short for 'você.'"

Tb / Tbm Also / Too

Pronunciation: tahm-BEYN

"Eu tb te amo. (I love you too.) Short for 'também.'"

Pq Why / Because

Pronunciation: por-KEH

"Pq vc não respondeu? (Why did you not respond?) Short for 'por que' or 'porque.'"

Qdo When

Pronunciation: KWAN-doo

"Qdo vc chega? (When do you arrive?) Short for 'quando.'"

Hj Today

Pronunciation: OH-zhee

"Vamos sair hj? (Shall we go out today?) Short for 'hoje.'"

Amnh Tomorrow

Pronunciation: ah-mah-NYAH

"Te vejo amnh. (See you tomorrow.) Short for 'amanhã.'"

Romantic Texting Slang

Sdds I miss you / Missing

Pronunciation: sow-DAH-deez

"Muitas sdds de vc. (I miss you so much.) Short for 'saudades.'"

Tmj We are together / I have got your back

Pronunciation: tah-MOH ZHOON-tohs

"Pode contar comigo, tmj. (You can count on me, I have got your back.) Short for 'tamo junto.'"

Mozão / Mozi Babe / Sweetie

Pronunciation: moh-ZOWN / MOH-zee

"Oi mozi, td bem? (Hey babe, everything okay?) Affectionate terms derived from 'meu amor.'"

S2 Heart / Love

Pronunciation: ess-DOYZ

"Te amo s2. (I love you, heart.) The S and 2 together look like a heart shape."

Common Expressions

🇧🇷

Emoji and Sticker Culture

Brazilians love using emojis and stickers extensively. A message without any emoji might seem cold or distant. When in doubt, add a heart ❤️ or a smiling face to warm up your texts.

Kkkk Hahaha (laughing)

Pronunciation: kah-KAH-kah-KAH

"Vc é muito engraçado kkkk. (You are so funny hahaha.) Multiple k's indicate intensity of laughter."

Rsrs / Rs Haha (softer laugh)

Pronunciation: heh-HEH

"Que fofo rsrs. (How cute haha.) Short for 'risos' (laughs)."

Flw Bye / See you

Pronunciation: fah-LOW

"Flw, te amo! (Bye, I love you!) Short for 'falou' used as a casual goodbye="/learn/en/pt/portuguese-good-morning-goodnight-phrases/">goodbye."

Blz Okay / Cool / Alright

Pronunciation: beh-LEH-zah

"Blz, te encontro às 8. (Alright, I will meet you at 8.) Short for 'beleza.'"

Vlw Thanks

Pronunciation: vah-LEW

"Vlw por tudo, amor. (Thanks for everything, love.) Short for 'valeu.'"

Questions and Responses

Cê tá onde? Where are you?

Pronunciation: seh tah ON-jee

"Amor, cê tá onde? (Love, where are you?) 'Cê' is a very casual spoken form of 'você.'"

Bora? Shall we go? / Let us do it?

Pronunciation: BOH-rah

"Cinema hj? Bora? (Movies today? Let us go?) Short for 'vamos embora.'"

Pdp Sure thing / No problem

Pronunciation: poh-dee pah

"Te busco às 7? Response: Pdp. (I will pick you up at 7? Response: Sure thing.) Short for 'pode pá.'"

Numbers and Time

1 bj / Bjs A kiss / Kisses

Pronunciation: OOM BEH-zhoo / BEH-zhohs

"Te vejo amnh. 1 bj! (See you tomorrow. A kiss!) Short for 'beijo' or 'beijos.'"

Abs Hugs

Pronunciation: ah-BRAH-sohs

"Bjs e abs. (Kisses and hugs.) Short for 'abraços.'"

D+ Too much / A lot

Pronunciation: deh-MAH-eez

"Te amo d+. (I love you so much.) The 'D+' represents 'demais.'"

Tips for Texting Your Partner

Start slow and learn the most common abbreviations first. When your partner uses something you do not understand, ask them. They will likely enjoy teaching you their texting style.

Mix formal Portuguese with casual slang to show range. Use full sentences for important conversations and slang for quick daily check-ins. This balance shows both respect and comfort with the language.

Remember that texting styles vary between Brazil and Portugal. Brazilian texts tend to use more abbreviations and informal language. Portuguese texts may be slightly more formal but still use many of the same shortcuts.

title: Portuguese Verb Conjugation Basics
content_html:
Portuguese verb conjugation might seem intimidating at first, but understanding the patterns makes it manageable. Portuguese verbs follow predictable rules based on their infinitive endings. Once you learn these patterns, you can conjugate thousands of verbs.

The Three Verb Groups

💕

Phrase to Learn

Falar, comer, partir

To speak, to eat, to leave

[ fah-LAHR, koh-MEHR, pahr-CHEER ]

These represent the three verb groups: -AR, -ER, and -IR verbs

Portuguese verbs are categorized by their infinitive endings. The -AR group is the largest and most commonearn/en/pt/portuguese-weather-vocabulary/">most commonearn/en/pt/portuguese-shopping-vocabulary/">most commonearn/en/pt/portuguese-moving-in-together/">most commonearn/en/pt/portuguese-honeymoon-travel-phrases/">most commonearn/en/pt/portuguese-food-and-restaurant-vocabulary/">most commonearn/en/pt/portuguese-baby-pregnancy-vocabulary/">most commonearn/en/pt/portuguese-100-common-phrases-for-english-speakers/">most common. Understanding each group's conjugation pattern is essential for speaking Portuguese fluently.

🇧🇷

AR Verbs Dominate

About 90% of new verbs created in Portuguese follow the -AR pattern. This means mastering -AR conjugations gives you the biggest return on your learning investment.

First Group: AR Verbs

falar

to speak

eu falo
você/ele/ela fala
nós falamos
vocês/eles/elas falam
Amar To love

Pronunciation: ah-MAHR

"Eu amo você. (I love you.) Follows the same pattern as falar."

amar

to love

eu amo
você/ele/ela ama
nós amamos
vocês/eles/elas amam

Other common -AR verbs include: trabalhar (to work), estudar (to study), gostar (to like), morar (to live), comprar (to buy).

Second Group: ER Verbs

comer

to eat

eu como
você/ele/ela come
nós comemos
vocês/eles/elas comem
Beber To drink

Pronunciation: beh-BEHR

"Você bebe café? (Do you drink coffee?) Follows the -ER pattern."

beber

to drink

eu bebo
você/ele/ela bebe
nós bebemos
vocês/eles/elas bebem

Other common -ER verbs include: vender (to sell), aprender (to learn), escrever (to write), correr (to run).

Third Group: IR Verbs

partir

to leave/depart

eu parto
você/ele/ela parte
nós partimos
vocês/eles/elas partem
Abrir To open

Pronunciation: ah-BREER

"Ela abre a porta. (She opens the door.)"

abrir

to open

eu abro
você/ele/ela abre
nós abrimos
vocês/eles/elas abrem

Other common -IR verbs include: assistir (to watch), decidir (to decide), dividir (to divide), existir (to exist).

Essential Irregular Verbs

🇵🇹

The Most Important Irregulars

Some of the most frequently used Portuguese verbs are irregular. The good news is that you will use them so often that they become second nature quickly.

ser

to be (permanent)

eu sou
você/ele/ela é
nós somos
vocês/eles/elas são

estar

to be (temporary)

eu estou
você/ele/ela está
nós estamos
vocês/eles/elas estão

ter

to have

eu tenho
você/ele/ela tem
nós temos
vocês/eles/elas têm

ir

to go

eu vou
você/ele/ela vai
nós vamos
vocês/eles/elas vão

Tips for Mastering Conjugation

Practice with your partner by describing your daily routines. Use phrases like "Eu trabalho" (I work), "Nós comemos" (We eat), and "Você estuda" (You study). The more you use these forms in conversation, the more natural they become.

Focus on the most common verbs first. You will use ser, estar, ter, and ir in almost every conversation. Master these four before moving on to less frequent verbs.

Video calls with your partner's family can be nerve-wracking, especially in another language. These essential Portuguese phrases will help you navigate calls with in-laws and extended family with confidence.

Portuguese English Pronunciation
